Title: RNAi-suppression of barley caffeic acid O-methyltransferase modifies lignin despite redundancy in the gene family

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: lignin syringyl units; 5-hydroxyguaiacyl-containing lignin; p-coumaric acid ester-linked to cell walls; tricin (minor)
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: cell wall recalcitrance, straw quality, bioenergy/feed digestibility
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: stem straw cell walls; lignifying internodes
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: syringyl lignin branch and related 3/5-O-methylation steps in monolignol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: O-methylates 5-hydroxyconiferaldehyde to sinapaldehyde; also catalyzes O-methylation of caffeic acid in assay conditions and is implicated in methylation of related lignin intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: downstream branch step toward syringyl monolignol formation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms precursor of sinapyl alcohol and syringyl lignin; deficiency diverts flux to 5-OH-G incorporation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step for S-unit formation; not explicitly established as sole rate-limiting step
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: expressed in lignifying tissues including stems, roots, lemma, palea and rachis; in internodes expression is higher at earlier developmental stages
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: high in stem internodes at earlier stages; detected in lignifying tissues
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: developmental stage of internodes; constitutive RNAi suppression under maize ubiquitin promoter
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: RNAi suppression of HvCOMT1 together with HvCOMT2 reduced COMT transcript, protein and O-methyltransferase activity, lowered lignin S/G ratio by ~50%, increased 5-OH-G incorporation 10-15-fold, reduced wall p-coumarate by ~50% in several lines, modestly reduced lignin content, and improved saccharification in some lines
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: phylogenetic/conserved-residue analysis; RT-qPCR; RNAi knockdown; enzyme assay; western blot; Klason lignin assay; thioacidolysis; 2D NMR; UHPLC-MS metabolite profiling; microarray transcript profiling; saccharification assay
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq dataset from Mascher et al. 2017 for expression support; microarray transcript profiling in COMTRNAi_4 and COMTRNAi_14; UHPLC-MS metabolomics
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: no obvious brown-midrib/gold-hull phenotype; no significant straw biomass penalty reported; lignin reduction was modest and saccharification gains were line-dependent
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: multiplex knockout or silencing of stem-expressed COMT paralogs could improve barley straw digestibility and bioenergy processing; redundancy means multiple family members should be targeted, potentially using CRISPR or tissue-specific strategies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: HvCOMT1 is one of three true barley COMTs identified by phylogeny plus conserved catalytic residues; HvCOMT1 is located on chromosome 7H and was used as the RNAi trigger fragment source
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HvCOMT1 was identified as a true barley COMT expressed in lignifying stem tissues. 2. RNAi targeting HvCOMT1 also suppressed HvCOMT2 and strongly reduced COMT protein/activity in stems. 3. Suppression shifted lignin away from syringyl units toward guaiacyl/5-OH-G structures, reduced p-coumarate association, and modestly improved saccharification.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: lignin syringyl units; 5-hydroxyguaiacyl-containing lignin; p-coumaric acid ester-linked to cell walls; tricin (minor)
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: cell wall recalcitrance, straw quality, bioenergy/feed digestibility
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: stem straw cell walls; lignifying internodes
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: syringyl lignin branch and related O-methylation steps in monolignol biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Likely O-methylates 5-hydroxyconiferaldehyde to sinapaldehyde in lignin biosynthesis; despite previous suggestion as flavone-specific O-methyltransferase, sequence and phylogeny support COMT function
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: downstream branch step toward syringyl monolignol formation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms precursor of sinapyl alcohol and syringyl lignin
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key contributor to S-unit formation in redundant stem COMT system
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: expressed in lignifying tissues including stems, roots, lemma, palea and rachis; high in earlier internode stages with much stronger developmental decline than HvCOMT1
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: detected in stem internodes, especially early stages
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: developmental stage of internodes; constitutive RNAi suppression under maize ubiquitin promoter
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: co-suppression of HvCOMT2 with HvCOMT1 contributed to reduced COMT activity/protein and altered lignin composition/structure in stems
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: phylogenetic/conserved-residue analysis; RT-qPCR; RNAi co-suppression; western blot; enzyme assay; lignin chemistry analyses
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq dataset from Mascher et al. 2017 for expression support; microarray transcript profiling; UHPLC-MS metabolomics
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: same line-level tradeoffs as HvCOMT1; redundancy complicates complete suppression
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: should be co-targeted with HvCOMT1 for effective lignin engineering in barley stems
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: HvCOMT2 is on chromosome 3H; previously proposed to be a flavone-specific OMT but reclassified here as a true COMT based on conserved residues and phylogenetic placement
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HvCOMT2 is a true barley COMT paralog expressed in lignifying tissues. 2. It was co-silenced by the HvCOMT1 RNAi construct, with 5-40-fold transcript reduction. 3. Functional redundancy with HvCOMT1 likely underlies the absence of a visible brown-midrib phenotype despite substantial lignin remodeling.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: putative COMT step in lignin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Predicted COMT-type O-methylation based on conserved residues and phylogeny, but not functionally tested in stem lignification in this study
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: downstream branch step toward syringyl monolignol formation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: predominantly expressed in roots and embryos; no expression detected in sampled stem internodes
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: root/embryo predominant; absent from studied internodes
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: maize ubiquitin promoter, constitutive RNAi construct had sequence similarity to HvCOMT3 but stem expression was undetectable
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: identified as a true barley COMT family member, but not implicated in the stem lignin phenotype because no stem internode expression was detected
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: phylogenetic/conserved-residue analysis; RT-qPCR; RNA-seq expression support
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq dataset from Mascher et al. 2017
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: may be relevant for tissue-specific engineering outside stems, but not a demonstrated determinant of stem lignin phenotype here
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: HvCOMT3 was identified as a true COMT on chromosome 6H, but it is not a primary experimental driver of the reported stem lignin changes
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HvCOMT3 is a third true COMT paralog in barley. 2. It is predominantly expressed in roots and embryos rather than stems. 3. It was not detected in internodes and therefore is unlikely to explain the stem lignin phenotype analyzed in the RNAi lines.
